coredata.dc:title: Sensitive dual-mode sensing platform for Amyloid β detection: Combining dual Z-scheme heterojunction enhanced photoelectrochemistry analysis and dual-wavelength ratiometric electrochemiluminescence strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Bu, Yuwei
Wang, Kun
Yang, Xiaoyan
Nie, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: As a tumor biomarker, the accumulation of amyloid β oligomers (Aβo) in the brain has been suggested as a key feature in the pathogenesis and progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD). In this work, we designed a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) and electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) dual-mode biosensor to achieve ultra-sensitive detection of Aβo. Specifically, the electrode surface modified Carbon Dots (C Dots) and the electrodeposited polyaniline (PANI) film formed a Z-scheme heterojunction reversing the photocurrent signal, and then the Aβo specific recognition peptide was attached to the surface via amide bonding between the amino group of PANI and carbonyl group of peptide. After that, in the presence of CdTe labeled specific recognition aptamer for Aβ (CdTe-Apt), Aβo was captured to construct a sandwich-type biosensor and exhibited a significantly enhanced cathodic photocurrent response because the formed dual Z-scheme heterojunction promoted charge separation efficiency. Interestingly, the proposed biosensor also caused a ratiometric change in the ECL intensity at 555 nm and 640 nm. Therefore, the developed biosensor achieved dual-mode detection of Aβo, where the PEC detection range of Aβo was from 10 fM to 0.1 μM (with a detection limit of 4.27 fM) and the ECL method provided a linear detection range of 10 fM to 10 nM (with a detection limit of 6.41 fM). The stability and reliability of the experimental results indicate that this has been a promising biosensing pattern and could be extended to the analysis of other biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode biosensor
Z-Scheme heterojunction
Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Photoelectrochemistry
Amyloid β

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the developed biosensor
Selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and antifouling of the designed biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of C Dots (inset: corresponding HRTEM image); (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of C Dots; (C) TEM image of CdTe QDs (inset: corresponding HRTEM image); (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdTe QDs.

(A) Photocurrent responses and (B) Nyquist diagrams for (a) ITO, (b) C Dots/ITO, (c) PANI/C Dots/ITO, (d) peptide/PANI/C Dots/ITO, (e) CdTe-Apt/Aβ/peptide/PANI/C Dots/ITO; (C) ECL intensity for (a) C Dots/Au, (b) PANI/C Dots/Au, (c) peptide/PANI/C Dots/Au, (d) Aβ/peptide/PANI/C Dots/Au, (e) CdTe-Apt/Aβ/peptide/PANI/C Dots/Au; (D) XPS full spectrum of PANI/C Dots electrodes; (E) XPS full spectrum of peptide/PANI/C Dots electrodes; (F) Band structure of CdTe QDs by DFT calculations.

Possible electron transfers pathways of (A) C Dots/ITO; (B) PANI/C Dots/ITO; and (C) the proposed biosensor; (D) UV–vis absorption of CdTe QDs (red region) and ECL emission spectrum of C Dots (blue region).

(A) PEC responses of the constructed biosensor for Aβo detection after incubation in different concentrations; and (B) the corresponding calibration curve; (C) ECL intensity at 555 nm and 640 nm of ratiometric biosensor at different Aβo concentrations; (D) Job curves of the ECL intensity and the logarithm of Aβo concentration (blue: 555 nm; red: 649 nm); (E) the corresponding linear relationship of acceptor and donor ratios; (F) Specificity test of the developed dual-mode biosensor in PEC analysis; (G) PEC stability of the developed dual-mode biosensor under continuous 17 cycles of measurements; (H) Specificity test of the developed dual-mode biosensor in ECL analysis; (I) ECL stability of the developed dual-mode biosensor under continuous 17 cycles of measurements. Error bars = SD (n = 3).

Analysis of antifouling performance: (A) bare electrode, (B) PANI/electrode, and (C) peptide/PANI/electrode (Scale bar is 1000 μm); (D) bare electrode, (E) PANI/electrode, and (F) peptide/PANI/electrode (Scale bar is 100 μm).
(A) Synthesis route diagram of C Dots; (B) Schematic illustration of the dual-mode biosensor detection of Aβ.
Recovery determination of Aβo with the dual-mode biosensor (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: Average value from three successive determinations (1–4 were measured by PEC; ⅰ-ⅳ were measured by ECL).

head.ce:title: Sensitive dual-mode sensing platform for Amyloid β detection: Combining dual Z-scheme heterojunction enhanced photoelectrochemistry analysis and dual-wavelength ratiometric electrochemiluminescence strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a tumor biomarker, the accumulation of amyloid β oligomers (Aβo) in the brain has been suggested as a key feature in the pathogenesis and progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD). In this work, we designed a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) and electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) dual-mode biosensor to achieve ultra-sensitive detection of Aβo. Specifically, the electrode surface modified Carbon Dots (C Dots) and the electrodeposited polyaniline (PANI) film formed a Z-scheme heterojunction reversing the photocurrent signal, and then the Aβo specific recognition peptide was attached to the surface via amide bonding between the amino group of PANI and carbonyl group of peptide. After that, in the presence of CdTe labeled specific recognition aptamer for Aβ (CdTe-Apt), Aβo was captured to construct a sandwich-type biosensor and exhibited a significantly enhanced cathodic photocurrent response because the formed dual Z-scheme heterojunction promoted charge separation efficiency. Interestingly, the proposed biosensor also caused a ratiometric change in the ECL intensity at 555 nm and 640 nm. Therefore, the developed biosensor achieved dual-mode detection of Aβo, where the PEC detection range of Aβo was from 10 fM to 0.1 μM (with a detection limit of 4.27 fM) and the ECL method provided a linear detection range of 10 fM to 10 nM (with a detection limit of 6.41 fM). The stability and reliability of the experimental results indicate that this has been a promising biosensing pattern and could be extended to the analysis of other biomarkers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with global aging, age-related dementia will affect more than 55 million people, causing a heavy economic burden on individuals and society (Aisen et al., 2022; Bellenguez et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023). As the most common type of dementia, Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an irreversible, chronic degenerative disease of the central nervous system that poses a serious threat to human health (Alzheimer's, 2018; Meda et al., 1995; Zheng et al., 2016). Although the pathogenesis of AD is not yet clear, a series of studies have suggested that β-amyloid peptide (Aβ) aggregation may be relevant to the pathological features and clinical diagnosis of AD (Han et al., 2017; Nichols et al., 2022; Panza et al., 2019; Qiang et al., 2017; Viola et al., 2015). Therefore, the development of efficient and accurate assays for the detection of Aβ aggregation has practical implications for the early diagnosis of AD.

So far, various methods have been implemented for Aβ detection, including surface-based fluorescence intensity distribution analysis (sFIDA) (Kravchenko et al., 2017), photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor (Bu et al., 2022), electrochemical (EC) biosensor (Veloso et al., 2014), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor (Tan et al., 2023), surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor (Yi et al., 2016), fluorescent (FL) biosensor (Xia et al., 2016), and enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) (Mehta et al., 2020). However, the single detection method provides fast and convenience at the same time, the detection results may be interfered by the complex detection environment, co-existing similar characteristics of the substance. On the basis of this background, dual-mode sensing platforms with independent signal outputs have become the most reliable and effective strategy for constructing biosensors (Mei et al., 2018; Ye et al., 2021). Dual-mode biosensors, such as PEC-photothermal (Li et al., 2022b), PEC-EC (Lu et al., 2019), PEC-ECL (Zheng et al., 2023), ECL-colorimetry (Hu et al., 2021), and FL-ECL (Jiao et al., 2021), and so on, take advantage of two independent signal comparisons and mutual verification to overcome the shortcoming of a high false rate of single signal output and dramatically enhances the reliability of test results. Among them, PEC-ECL dual-mode sensing assay has gained much attention because of its advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, fast response speed, and easy operation (Çakıroğlu et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2015). Especially, both the PEC method and the ECL technique used the same electrochemical instrumentation, which improves the accuracy of the test results while reducing costs and simplifying operational procedures.

Aptamers screened from random single-stranded nucleic acid sequence libraries employing systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) technology have attracted considerable attention because of specific and high affinity identified targets (Nguyen et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2014). Furthermore, the advantages of rapid synthesis, small size, great stability, ease of modification, and reusability allow aptamers to be a powerful tool for disease diagnosis and drug delivery (Joe et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2016). For example, Tsukakoshi et al. reported the use of aptamer blotting to competitively screen for aptamers that specifically recognize Aβo (Tsukakoshi et al., 2012), and the excellent affinity provided a promising analytical method for detecting Aβo. Whiten et al. developed a way to characterize endogenous protein aggregates within cells at the nanoscale by using amyloid-specific aptamers (Whiten et al., 2018). Chen et al. reported an ultrasensitive “sandwich” electrochemical immunosensor based on aptamer-target-antibody with a detection limit as low as 0.4 pM for Aβo (Chen et al., 2022). Zhang et al. designed a label-free electrochemical aptamer biosensor to achieve sensitivity detection of Aβo by optimizing the stem-loop structure with an adjustable detection range (Zhang et al., 2019). In our previous work, a label-free and signal-dependent ECL aptamer sensing strategy was proposed for the detection of Aβ, and the biosensor exhibited good analytical performance with a linear detection range of 1.0 × 10−13 to 1.0 × 10−8 mol/L and a detection limit of 3.5 × 10−14 mol/L (Qin et al., 2019).

Herein, we developed a dual-mode sensing assay method combination with PEC and ECL for highly selective detection of Aβ aggregation, and a mutually non-interfering dual signal output path was successfully constructed for responding to the target Aβ. In particular, CdTe QDs altered the migration path of photoinduced carriers in the presence of the targeted Aβ and enhanced the signal intensity of PEC. Meanwhile, the high resonance energy transfer efficiency exhibited between the donor (C Dots) and the acceptor (CdTe), and the ratio of ECL intensity at 555 nm and 640 nm was linearly related to the logarithmic value of Aβ concentration. The established sensing platforms have provided highly sensitive and reliable assays in human serum systems, demonstrating potential applicability in early disease diagnosis.

Citric acid (CA), urea (CH4N2O), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), aniline (C6H5NH2), perchloric acid (HClO4), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), and ascorbic acid (AA) were provided by Shanghai State Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Poly (sodium-p-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fluorescein isothiocyanate-labeled BSA (FITC-BSA) was purchased from Sangon Biological Engineering Technology and Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human serum samples were obtained from Qingdao Eighth People's Hospital (Qingdao, China). Other regents were of analytical purity and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm), which was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (USA).

Peptide: Suc-CPPPPDKDKDKDKKLVFF (purity >95%) was obtained from Bank peptide Biological Technology Co. Led. (Hefei, China), which could recognize low molecular weight Aβ40 oligomers (Fig. S1) (Hatai et al., 2017).

The Aβ40 and aptamer (purified by HPLC) used in this work were ordered from Sangon Biological Engineering Technology and Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with the following sequences.
Aβ40: DAEFRHDSGYEVHHQKLVFFAEDVGSNKGAILGLMVGGVV
Aptamer：5′–NH2–GCCTGTGGTGTTGGGGCGGGTGCG-3′

The aptamer was screened by Tsukakoshi et al. which could selectively recognize Aβ40 oligomers, and the K d value for Aβ40 oligomers obtained by enzyme-linked oligonucleotide assay (ELONA) analysis was 25 ± 6 nM (Tsukakoshi et al., 2012). The aptamer was heat-treated at 95 °C for 5 min before use, and then transferred to 4 °C for cooling. In this work, the concentration of Aβ was defined as the equivalent concentration to the corresponding Aβ monomer concentration.

The general procedure for preparing of C Dots was based on the reported literature with appropriate modifications (Qu et al., 2012). Typically, 3.0 g of citric acid, 3.0 g of urea were added to 10 mL of ultrapure water in a crucible to form a clear solution. The solution was then heated in a household 750 W microwave oven for 5 min to obtain a dark brown aggregated solid. Subsequently, the solid product was transferred to a vacuum oven and heated at 60 °C for 1 h to remove residual small molecules. The brown aqueous solution obtained was purified by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min and stored at 4 °C for further use (Scheme 1 A).

CdTe QDs were synthesized from previous methods with appropriate modifications (Fan et al., 2014; Zou et al., 2008). First, 0.3 mmol of CdCl2 and 0.51 mmol of MPA were mixed in 60 mL of solution in a three-necked flask and 1.0 M NaOH was added with stirring to adjust the pH of the solution until turned clarified. Subsequently, the solution was deaerated with N2 for 30 min. After sequentially adding 60 mg of NaBH4 and 67 mg of Na2TeO3, the mixed solution was heated to 100 °C and refluxed under N2 protection for 6 h to obtain the CdTe solution. Finally, the product was purified twice by centrifugation with the equal volume of ethanol, and the precipitate was dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The process of the designed dual-mode biosensor platform construction is shown in Scheme 1B. Prior to modification, the ITO electrode was ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and ultrapure water in sequence for 15 min, and then was dried at electric constant temperature blast drying oven. First, 20 μL of 0.75 mg mL−1 C Dots solution was dropped onto the ITO electrode and dried at room temperature. Next, PANI electrodeposition was carried out at a constant current density of 0.01 mA cm−2 for 5 min using the galvanostatic technique of chronopotentiometry (CP). After activating the –COOH of the peptide with EDC (0.1 M) and NHS (0.025 M) for 1 h at room temperature, 20 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 peptide solution was immobilized onto the PANI/C Dots/ITO electrode via amide bonding and incubated for 6 h at 37 °C. Finally, 10 μL of different concentrations of Aβo solution and 10 μL of CdTe-Apt solution were successively dropped on the electrode and incubated for 6 h at 37 °C. Specifically, the electrode surface was washed using PBS buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 7.4) after stepwise modification for the next step.

The photocurrent test was performed in PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M AA. The exciting light source was a 350 W Xe lamp, and switched on and off every 10 s with the potential bias of 0 V. The ECL measurement was carried out in a traditional electrochemical detection three-electrode system with a modified gold electrode as the working electrode and in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. In particular, the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage was −700 V, and the continuous potential range was from −1.5 to 0 V at a scan rate of 0.2 V·s−1. All tests were performed at room temperature and repeated three times.

The morphology and size of the prepared C Dots were characterized utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As observed from Fig. 1 A, the image showed good dispersion of C Dots with uniform morphology and the diameter of nanospheres was about 3.1 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis spectra were used to further confirm the synthesis of C Dots. The result showed that C Dots aqueous solution had a broad absorption spectrum with peaks concentrated at 340 nm and 405 nm consistent with the literature (Qu et al., 2012). As clearly shown in Fig. 1C, TEM and HRTEM image (inset) were carried out to reflect the crystal structure of CdTe QDs. The lattice fringes were clearly visible with a lattice spacing distance of 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of CdTe (Vijayaraj et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 1D, UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared CdTe QDs exhibited a wide visible light response from 350 nm to 650 nm.

The photocurrent intensity, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) intensity were effective characterization methods to verify successful biosensor construction. Fig. 2 A exhibits a series of PEC behaviors in the biosensor construction process. Compared with the bare ITO electrode (Fig. 2A, curve a), the C Dots modified electrode obtained enhanced anodic photocurrent response, which could be attributed to the superior photoelectric activity of C Dots (Fig. 2A, curve b). Inversion of the photocurrent signal of the electrode after electrodeposition of PANI (Fig. 2A, curve c), because the formation of a heterojunction structure shifted the flow of electrons at the electrode (Ekande and Kumar, 2021). Subsequently, the non-conductive peptide was modified on the electrode and the photocurrent intensity decreased (Fig. 2A, curve d). When Aβ and CdTe-Apt were successively connected to the electrode, the biosensor exhibited a significantly enhanced photocurrent signal, which could be ascribed mainly to energy level matching effect of CdTe QDs (Fig. 2A, curve e). Meanwhile, EIS measurements were recorded during the step-by-step modification in Fig. 2B. After modification of the electrode with C Dots, the C Dots/ITO electrode (Fig. 2B, curve b) displayed a slightly lower charge-transfer resistance (Ret) than the bare ITO electrode (Fig. 2B, curve a). Subsequently, the electrodeposited PANI film with excellent electrical conductivity exhibited furthermore reduced Ret (Fig. 2B, curve c). With the aptamer was immobilized on the electrode, the weak conductivity of nucleic acid molecules led to a significant increase in Ret value (Fig. 2B, curve d). The electrode surface showed a smaller semicircle after connecting CdTe-Apt, which was due to the improved charge separation efficiency (Fig. 2B, curve e). Besides, the intensity of ECL was recorded in Fig. 2C during the biosensor construction. First, with the modification of the C Dots, the electrode exhibited a strong ECL signal intensity (Fig. 2C, curve a). After PANI, peptides and Aβ were sequentially immobilized onto the electrode, the ECL intensity of the electrode gradually decreased because of the site-blocking effect and the blocked electron transfer of the protein (Fig. 2C, curve b, c, and d) (Ai et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022a). Finally, when the developed biosensor was incubated with CdTe-Apt, the ECL intensity was substantially weakened (Fig. 2C, curve e). This phenomenon was attributed to the RET-ECL effect between C Dots and CdTe QDs. All the above results demonstrated the success construction of the proposed dual-mode biosensor.

After PANI deposition and peptide incubation, overall XPS spectra were further measured to demonstrate the alteration of the sensing interface. As shown in Fig. 2D, the survey spectra of PANI/C Dots electrodes displayed the presence of C, N, O, and Cl elements. Among them, the peaks of C, N and O could originate from PANI and C Dots, and the peak of Cl2p appear to be doped during the electrodeposition process. Subsequently, the elemental species on the electrode surface changed after the peptide incubation (Fig. 2E). The intensity of the peak of Cl2p decreased with the appearance of two new peaks of S at 164 eV (S2p) and 228 eV (S2s). This phenomenon could be ascribed to the cysteine in the peptide, indicating the peptide is successfully modified on the interface (Song et al., 2021). Both electrochemical measures and XPS spectra indicated the successful assembly of the biosensor.

The possible transport mechanism for photogenerated electrons is discussed in Fig. 3 . The valence band (VB), conduction band (CB), and band gap (Eg) values of the C Dots were measured by the valence band energy spectrum and Tauc plots, respectively (see the Supporting Information, Fig. S2). As depicted in Fig. 2F, the VB, CB, and Eg date of CdTe QDs were obtained by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. In addition, the Eg value of PANI were collected from the literature (Sharma and Khare, 2018; Sharma et al., 2021). Under the irradiation of excitation light, the photogenerated electrons at the C Dots are transferred from valence band to conduction band and subsequently flow to the ITO electrode, resulting in an anodic photocurrent (Fig. 3A). After deposition of PANI, a suitable band gap matching structure between the C Dots and PANI forms a Z-scheme heterojunction. It could be seen from Fig. 3B, the electrons originally flowing to the electrode surface are transferred to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PANI, and eventually consumed by the dissolved O2 to generate ·O2 − (Xu et al., 2022). Thus, the polarity of the photocurrent is switched and a cathodic photocurrent signal is obtained. With the presence of Aβ, CdTe is introduced into the sensing system, and the electron transport mechanism is adjusted consequently. As displayed in Fig. 3C, the CB potential of CdTe is close to the VB potential of PANI, and the short transmission distance promotes the transfer efficiency of electrons from the CB of CdTe to the VB of PANI. Consequently, dual Z-scheme heterogeneous structures are formed through this electron delivery pattern, which contribute greatly to the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and enlarge the photocurrent intensity (Wu et al., 2023).

The principle of the ratiometric biosensor could be implemented based on the ECL-RET between C Dots and CdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 3D, the overlap of the ECL emission spectrum of C Dots with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CdTe within the absorption range centered at 555 nm revealed the possibility of the occurrence of ECL-RET. In addition, Fig. S3 depicts the emission band in the 450–700 nm range. Compared with its own fluorescence emission spectrum (FL), the maximum ECL emission spectra of both C Dots and CdTe are slightly red-shifted (about 30 nm). Owing to the different emission states of ECL and FL, the ECL of CdTe quantum dots and C Dots is mainly generated by surface state leaps due to the presence of passivated incomplete surface traps, while fluorescence tends more toward the core state (Myung et al., 2003). The energy band gap of the surface state is smaller than that of the core state, and thus the wavelength of ECL emission is higher than that of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the developed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Notably, suitable experimental conditions were vital for the performance of the designed biosensor, therefore the influence of a series of experimental conditions was discussed in Fig. S4. Under optimized experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the proposed biosensor was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the photocurrent intensity of the developed biosensor enhanced with the increased Aβ concentration. Fig. 4B depicts an excellent linear relationship between the photocurrent signal and the Aβ concentrations ranging from 10 fM to 0.1 μM. The regression equation was expressed as Y = −51.55 lgC
Aβ – 875.41 (R
2 = 0.992), and the detection limit was calculated to be 4.27 fM. As shown in Fig. 4C, with the concentration of Aβ increased, the ECL intensity of C Dots became smaller and the ECL intensity of CdTe QDs turned larger, indicating the successful construction of an ECL-RET-based biosensor for the analysis of Aβ. It could be seen in Fig. 4D, the ECL-RET model biosensor exhibited good linear detection of Aβ in the concentration range from 10 fM to 10 nM, with the linear equations of Y = −1072.27 lgC
Aβ – 5664.83 (R
2 = 0.994) and Y = 711.49 lgC
Aβ + 10,879.10 (R
2 = 0.994), respectively. In addition, we further detected Aβ via calculation of the ratio of ECL intensity of CdTe QDs and C Dots employing ratiometric assay, and the linear equation was Y = 0.22 lgC
Aβ + 3.08 (R
2 = 0.989) with a detection limit of 6.41 fM (Fig. 4E). Compared with other previous studies, this developed biosensor performed excellent potential and analytical performance for ultrasensitive detection of Aβ (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and antifouling of the designed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the selectivity of the designed biosensor, a series of interfering proteins and different aggregation states of Aβ were introduced for interference testing. As depicted in Fig. 4F and H, significant signal distinctions between the target analyte Aβo with non-specific proteins and other aggregated states of Aβ confirm the specificity of the constructed dual-mode biosensor. Furthermore, we monitored the photocurrent intensity change under 17 “off-on-off” cycles to study the dynamic stability. As could be seen in Fig. 4G, with over 300s of testing the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.99%, indicating that the biosensor has good stability. On the other hand, the stability of the ECL detection technique was assessed by monitoring the signal intensity for more than 15 cycles of continuous potential scanning with 0.1 nM Aβo. As illustrated in Fig. 4I, the relative standard deviation of the ECL intensity values for C Dots and CdTe were 2.69% and 1.76%, respectively. These results also demonstrated the excellent stability of the dual-mode biosensor. Fig. S5 exhibits the excellent reproducibility of the biosensor in five different batches of experiments, with the relative standard deviation is 1.43%.

Moreover, the antifouling performance was also explored via fluorescent protein (FITC-BSA) adsorption testing. Typically, 0.2 mg mL−1 TITC-BSA solution was added onto the bare electrode, PANI/electrode and peptide/PANI/electrode, respectively. And then incubated at 37 °C for 3 h to allow protein adsorption. After rinsing off the no-absorbed FITC-BSA solution, observation of protein adsorption behavior at different interfaces employed fluorescence microscopy. As could be seen in Fig. 5 A and D, the bare electrode surface adsorbed a large amount of TITC-BSA protein and showed the strongest fluorescence intensity. After deposition of PANI film, the fluorescence intensity of the electrode surface gradually decreased (Fig. 5B and E). It could be related to the dense structure of the electrode surface film after deposition (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 5C and F, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased to a weak degree with further modification of the peptide. The results demonstrate that the peptide-modified electrode has superior resistance to protein adsorption.

Adaptation to complex real sample detection environments is an essential factor in biosensor detection results. The feasibility of the constructed dual-mode biosensor for clinical application was further investigated through the standard addition method. The human serum samples were isolated from the whole blood centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. In order to cover the wide linear detection range of the proposed protocol, the serum samples were diluted 100-fold with PBS solution (0.01 M) before use. Different concentrations of Aβo were added into the pretreated serum samples. As listed in Table 1 , the recovery was in the range of 97.64%–104.3% with a relative standard deviation of 2.26%–5.82%. These results demonstrated that the developed PEC-ECL biosensor could mutually validate the conclusions reached by the two methods as well as increase the reliability and accuracy of the measurements, which has potential application for sensitive and specific detecting of Aβ in clinical analysis.

In conclusion, a novel sandwich-type biosensor was constructed by using the composite of the C Dots and PANI film as a matrix and the aptamer labeled CdTe QDs as a signal modulate. With the following advantages: (ⅰ) the dual Z-scheme electron transport structure significantly enhances the carrier separation efficiency and obtains large and stable photocurrent signals; (ⅱ) the dual-wavelength ratiometric sensing strategy shows good ECL-RET efficiency and enables sensitive and selective detection of Aβo; (ⅲ) the designed dual-mode sensing platform utilizes dual signal outputs from PEC and ECL to further validate the reliability of the detection results. Therefore, this work provides a promising way for detecting tumor biomarkers and exhibits potential applications in clinical diagnosis. It also provides ideas and attempts for the construction of other multimodal biosensing platforms.

Yuwei Bu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Kun Wang: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Xiaoyan Yang: Conceptualization, Writing–Reviewing and Editing, Supervision. Guangming Nie: Validation, Data curation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciated the finance supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973102), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019MB051, ZR2019MB067), Innovation Ability Improvement Project of Science and Technology Small and Medium-Size Enterprise in Shandong Province (2022TSGC1121).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2023.115507.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ai et al., 2021
